You are an independent skeptic verifying another agent's investigation of the RBK "Lode" subsystem (fetched-side artifact capture into GAR), in the repo at the current working directory. Do NOT trust their summary — RE-READ the cited files yourself.

Their area: vertical (vertical shape / granularity)

Their findings (JSON):
[
  {
    "claim": "The spine (rblds_Spine.sh) already provides for free: recipe compilation (|-delimited step rows -> Build steps JSON), substitution-coverage validation, the full Build envelope (mason SA, TETHER pool, regime timeout, automapSubstitutions, CLOUD_LOGGING_ONLY), submit, poll-to-completion, and buildStepOutputs slot decode. It owns NO kind knowledge. So any new kind that captures by submitting a Cloud Build of skopeo/docker/python steps reusing _RBGL_* substitutions reuses ALL of this with zero spine change.",
    "evidence": "rblds_Spine.sh:19-31 (docstring: 'Owns NO kind knowledge'), :118-236 zrbld_spine_dispatch composes+submits+polls, :244-258 zrbld_spine_extract; capture-domain constants spine-owned at :195 (mason SA), :201-202 (TETHER pool, timeout)",
    "confidence": "high"
  },
  {
    "claim": "The bole body (rbldb_Bole.sh) is genuinely thin: it owns only (a) a 2-row recipe naming rbgjl01/rbgjl02, (b) a jq substitutions blob of _RBGL_* registers, (c) host-side stamp mint + vessel-origin resolution, and (d) a per-member slot_n envelope extract loop. That is the per-kind NET-NEW surface a new vertical must rewrite. ~215 lines total, most of which is structure a new kind copies.",
    "evidence": "rbldb_Bole.sh:55-58 (recipe rows), :62-90 (subs blob), :104-138 (extract loop), :143-214 (rbld_ensconce host orchestration: vessel resolve, single-origin enforce, stamp mint)",
    "confidence": "high"
  },
  {
    "claim": "TOOL kind (verb fetter): payload is native layered OCI; member cardinality is N (6 tool images today: gcloud, docker, alpine, syft, binfmt, skopeo); fetch tool is docker pull (not skopeo); trust grade is recorded-at-acquisition (digest captured from RepoDigests after pull, NOT verified against a published checksum). The capture logic ALREADY EXISTS as rbgji01-inscribe-mirror.sh (docker pull/tag/push loop emitting tool->digest JSON). Absorbing it onto the spine is mostly mechanical: rename _RBGN_* registers to _RBGL_*, wrap each tool as a Lode member, add the vouch envelope/push step. SMALL-to-MEDIUM.",
    "evidence": "rbgji01-inscribe-mirror.sh:22-29 (6-image MANIFEST), :43-54 (docker pull/tag/push), :47-48 (digest from RepoDigests = recorded-at-acquisition); rbfli_Inscribe.sh:31-126 (existing submit machinery the spine replaces); RBGC_LODE_TRUST_RECORDED at rbgc_Constants.sh:232",
    "confidence": "high"
  },
  {
    "claim": "RELIQUARY kind (verb conclave) is the SAME machinery as tool — it IS the current inscribe (rbfli_Inscribe.sh + rbgji01). 'reliquary' is an N-member date-cohort of exactly those tool images under one datestamp; the spec treats tool and reliquary as near-identical (RBSHR lists both as kind slots absorbing today's mirror/reliquary). The only divergence from tool is packaging cardinality framing (cohort-under-one-stamp vs per-tool member), and it carries the live 'inscribe' fork slated for cutover (GAR namespace rbi_rq -> rbi_ld). Tool and reliquary are the strongest candidates to GROUP — they share fetch tool (docker), trust grade (recorded), payload (native OCI), and source list.",
    "evidence": "RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc:69-73 ('build-time tool images (today mirror/reliquary machinery)... reliquary demotes from top-level noun to a kind enum slot — the date-cohort semantic survives'); acronyms.md:49 reserves RBLDR 'reliquary/conclave'; rbfli_Inscribe.sh:142-144 reliquary datestamp = r+YYMMDDHHMMSS (same cohort form bole uses with kind-letter)",
    "confidence": "high"
  },
  {
    "claim": "WSL kind (verb underpin) is the FARTHEST divergence from the bole/spine path. It fetches a rootfs tarball over plain HTTPS with a published SHA-256 — NOT an OCI registry pull. The spine's whole premise (skopeo/docker against a registry, _RBGL_* manifest tags, GAR->GAR retag, OCI digest as canonical identity) does not apply to a raw tarball. A new cloud step would need curl+sha256-verify (genuine verified-against-published, the only kind where the published-checksum branch is real), then wrap the opaque tarball as a FROM-scratch OCI artifact to land it in a Lode package. Member cardinality is 1 (one rootfs). This needs the most NET-NEW step logic and likely a new rbgjl0N step that shares almost nothing with rbgjl01-ensconce-capture. MEDIUM-to-LARGE, and structurally an outlier.",
    "evidence": "RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc:75 ('wsl kind delivers a project-controlled WSL rootfs tar'); rbgjs-skopeo-fingerprint.sh requires ORIGIN as docker:// ref (registry-only); rbgjl01:99-113 skopeo copy --all + GAR->GAR retag assumes registry source; no existing rootfs-over-HTTPS+published-SHA fetch anywhere in Tools/rbk except the abandoned github path (rbhr_GithubRemote.sh:92-93 curl+tar, no Lode wiring)",
    "confidence": "high"
  },
  {
    "claim": "PODVM kind (verb immure) is the LARGEST and most internally split. Payload is opaque blobs (CoreOS disk images / rootfs tarballs) wrapped as OCI — 'non-standard media types, custom disktype annotations, opaque blobs rather than composable layers'; standard docker/podman pull CANNOT retrieve them. Fetch tool is crane (manifest+blob navigation), not skopeo/docker. Cardinality is N across a fan-out: TWO quay families (machine-os-wsl 'mow' and machine-os 'mos') x multiple platform variants (arch+disktype). Trust grade is recorded-at-acquisition (provenance only reliably available at init time; brand-file captured post-hoc). The existing prototype (rbv_PodmanVM.sh) does crane manifest -> base64 entry decode -> per-platform crane blob -> repackage FROM-scratch -> push, and is GHCR-targeted and NEVER ported to GAR. Net-new body+cloud-step logic is large and shares little with bole beyond the spine envelope.",
    "evidence": "RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc:17 (opaque blobs, non-standard media types, crane/skopeo/oras below container abstraction), :23 (two families mow/mos with different build processes), :110 (blob-level extraction + multi-platform iteration); rbv_PodmanVM.sh:201-249 zrbv_process_image_type (crane manifest + base64 entry fan-out), :360-420 per-platform crane blob + FROM-scratch repackage, :21-26 GHCR not ported to GAR; acronyms.md:218 kind letters vw=podvm-wsl, vn=podvm-native (two distinct letters)",
    "confidence": "high"
  },
  {
    "claim": "Trust-grade split is a real two-way axis, not per-kind cosmetic: bole and wsl can be verified-against-published (bole via canonical OCI digest, wsl via published SHA-256); tool, reliquary, and podvm are recorded-at-acquisition (digest captured from what was pulled, no upstream-published reference to check). Both constants already exist; the envelope already carries trust_grade as a substitution. So trust grade is data, not new code per kind.",
    "evidence": "rbgc_Constants.sh:229-232 (two grades, comment: 'bole captures durable-upstream grade; podvm-* will carry recorded grade'); rbldb_Bole.sh:69 passes RBGC_LODE_TRUST_VERIFIED; rbgjl01:128 trust_grade is a substitution register",
    "confidence": "high"
  },
  {
    "claim": "All four remaining kinds reuse the host-side scaffolding bole established: stamp mint with kind-letter prefix (RBGC_LODE_KIND_* — only bole 'b' defined; t/r/w/vw/vn reserved but unwritten), the rbi_ld package-is-atomic-delete-unit layout, the rbi_vouch envelope + capture-file fact-multi pattern, and divine/banish/augur read verbs which are kind-agnostic. New constants per kind are trivial (a kind letter + reusing existing tag/trust constants).",
    "evidence": "rbgc_Constants.sh:217-219 (kind letters reserved, only BOLE defined), :209 RBGC_GAR_CATEGORY_LODES rbi_ld; rbldl_Lifecycle.sh:60 divine already enumerates by kind; acronyms.md:49 RBLDT/RBLDR/RBLDW/RBLDV reserved legend-only",
    "confidence": "high"
  }
]

Their recommendation_input:
Per-kind weight estimate (net-new body + cloud-step logic beyond pure spine reuse):

- tool (fetter): SMALL. Logic already exists (rbgji01 docker pull/tag/push). Mostly a rename/rewrap onto the spine + add vouch step. Native OCI, docker pull, recorded-at-acquisition, N members.
- reliquary (conclave): SMALL, and nearly identical to tool — same docker fetch, same recorded grade, same 6-image source, same datestamp-cohort. The divergence from tool is framing (cohort-under-one-stamp), not mechanism. Carries the inscribe fork to cut over.
- wsl (underpin): MEDIUM-LARGE. Structural outlier — HTTPS+published-SHA tarball fetch, NOT a registry pull. Needs a new cloud step (curl + sha256-verify + FROM-scratch wrap) that shares almost nothing with rbgjl01. It is, however, the one kind where verified-against-published is genuinely earned. Cardinality 1.
- podvm (immure): LARGE. Crane manifest/blob navigation, opaque non-layered blobs, two quay families (mow/mos) x platform fan-out, GHCR prototype never ported to GAR, two kind-letters (vw/vn). Most net-new logic by far.

Granularity recommendation:
1. GROUP tool + reliquary into ONE pace (or one pace with the second as a thin follow-on). They share fetch tool, trust grade, payload shape, and source list; reliquary is tool's date-cohort framing. Both absorb the same inscribe fork. Building them together avoids duplicating the docker-pull cloud step.
2. ONE pace for wsl. It diverges hard enough (HTTPS+published-SHA, new cloud step, opaque-tarball wrap) that it does not benefit from sharing a pace with the registry-pull kinds; but it is a single coherent vertical.
3. SPLIT podvm — recommend splitting along CAPTURE vs PLATFORM-SELECTION rather than native-vs-wsl. The crane manifest/blob capture machinery and the GAR-port of the FROM-scratch repackage are one body of work; the two-family + platform-variant fan-out (mow/mos selection, disktype/arch indexing, the vw/vn kind-letters) is a second, layerable concern. (Native-vs-wsl split is the weaker cut because both families share the identical crane capture path and differ only in which quay repo + variant set they enumerate.) At minimum podvm should be 2 paces; do not try to land it as one.

Net: roughly 3 build paces for the easy registry kinds + wsl (group tool/reliquary, separate wsl), and 2 paces for podvm = ~5 build paces, NOT one-per-kind (which would over-count the tool/reliquary near-duplication) and NOT a single grouped pace (which would bury the wsl/podvm divergence). The spine investment from BX pays off most for tool/reliquary (near-zero new machinery) and least for podvm (crane path is outside the spine's registry-pull assumptions but still rides the submit/poll envelope).

Independently check the LOAD-BEARING factual claims against the actual files. For each, return whether it holds (with a note citing what you found). Then — most important — state in `corrections` anything they got WRONG, any citation that's off, or anything they MISSED that would change the recommendation. If the recommendation stands as-is, set corrections to an empty string.

- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

15	# limitations under the License.
16	#
17	# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
18	#
19	# Recipe Bottle Lode - capture-assembly spine (guard-free cluster, sourced by
20	# rbld0_Lode): the data-driven Cloud Build composer shared by every Lode capture
21	# kind. Takes a recipe (ordered, pre-resolved step rows) plus an opaque
22	# substitutions blob, composes the Build resource, submits, polls, and decodes a
23	# step's buildStepOutputs slot. Owns NO kind knowledge: the recipe and the
24	# substitutions are data; the only kind-flavored build knob (the poll ceiling) is
25	# passed by the body. Capture-domain constants (the mason capture SA, the TETHER
26	# pool, the regime build timeout) are spine-owned, because every Lode capture
27	# fetches upstream bytes as mason over the network — that is what capture is, not
28	# a per-kind choice. Built on the rbfcb_ build primitives (write-script-body,
29	# wait-build-completion), which it calls rather than absorbs.
30	#
31	# Contract: RBSCJ "Capture Composition Contract".
32	
33	set -euo pipefail
34	
35	######################################################################
36	# Capture-assembly spine (zrbld_spine_*)
37	
38	# Internal: dispatch-time substitution-coverage check. A capture step reads
39	# automapped _RBGL_* substitution registers; a reference to a key the body's
40	# substitutions blob never defines expands to empty inside the build and corrupts
41	# the capture silently — the one composition fault that is neither guarded in-step
42	# (unlike the /workspace handoff, which the steps test -f for themselves) nor cheap
43	# to surface (it costs the whole cook). The _RBGL_* references in a step body ARE
44	# its substitution requires — no separate declaration to drift from the code — so
45	# this scans the include-expanded body for them on non-comment lines and returns 1
46	# at the first reference absent from the blob's keys, logging the offending
47	# register. Sentinel-free return-1 primitive, like the rbfcb_ build primitives the
48	# dispatch loop already rides; the caller buc_die's with the step identity.
49	#
50	# Coverage is flat by design (RBSCJ "Substitution-coverage check"): substitutions
51	# automap into every step, so there is no cross-step ordering; the /workspace
52	# inter-step channel keeps its own in-step guards and is out of scope.
53	#
54	# Args: keys_file expanded_body_file
55	zrbld_spine_validate() {
56	  local -r z_keys_file="${1:?Substitution keys file required}"
57	  local -r z_body_file="${2:?Expanded body file required}"
58	
59	  test -f "${z_keys_file}" || return 1
60	  test -f "${z_body_file}" || return 1
61	
62	  # Keys as newline-bounded text for builtin whole-line membership tests. Empty
63	  # keys (a blob declaring no registers) is legitimate — no test -n.
64	  local -r z_keys=$(<"${z_keys_file}")
65	  local -r z_keys_blob=$'\n'"${z_keys}"$'\n'
66	
67	  # Load the body, then iterate — the file is closed before the scan begins.
68	  local z_lines=()
69	  local z_line=""
70	  while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
71	    z_lines+=("${z_line}")
72	  done < "${z_body_file}"
73	
74	  local z_i=0
75	  local z_rest=""
76	  local z_ref=""
77	  for z_i in "${!z_lines[@]}"; do
78	    z_line="${z_lines[$z_i]}"
79	
80	    # Full-line comments only — a _RBGL_ token in a leading-# comment is
81	    # documentation, not a read; a trailing inline mention stays conservative.
82	    [[ ! "${z_line}" =~ ^[[:space:]]*# ]] || continue
83	
84	    # Each _RBGL_* token by repeated leftmost match; strip through the match so
85	    # the next token on the line surfaces.
86	    z_rest="${z_line}"
87	    while [[ "${z_rest}" =~ _RBGL_[A-Z0-9_]+ ]]; do
88	      z_ref="${BASH_REMATCH[0]}"
89	      case "${z_keys_blob}" in
90	        *$'\n'"${z_ref}"$'\n'*) ;;
91	        *) buc_log_args "Uncovered substitution register: ${z_ref}"; return 1 ;;
92	      esac
93	      z_rest="${z_rest#*"${z_ref}"}"
94	    done
95	  done
96	
97	  return 0
98	}
99	
100	# Internal: compose, submit, and poll a Lode capture Cloud Build from a recipe
101	# plus an opaque substitutions blob.
102	#
103	# A recipe row is a |-delimited 4-tuple: script_path|builder_image|id|entrypoint
104	#   - script_path:   absolute path to the step script, pre-resolved by the body
105	#                    (the spine owns no steps-directory knowledge)
106	#   - builder_image: the step's builder image ref (colons allowed — hence the
107	#                    | delimiter, since image tags/digests contain colons)
108	#   - id:            the Cloud Build step id
109	#   - entrypoint:    bash | sh | python3 — selects the composed shebang line
110	#
111	# The substitutions file holds a JSON object the spine slots verbatim into the
112	# Build envelope's `substitutions` field; the spine reads no key from it. The
113	# envelope shape (serviceAccount, options.automapSubstitutions, options.logging,
114	# options.pool, timeout) is the spine's; the values that vary by capture domain
115	# (mason SA, TETHER pool, regime timeout) are spine-owned constants.
116	#
117	# Args: token label poll_ceiling subs_file temp_prefix recipe_row...
118	zrbld_spine_dispatch() {
119	  zrbld_sentinel
120	
121	  local -r z_token="${1:?Token required}";                  shift
122	  local -r z_label="${1:?Label required}";                  shift
123	  local -r z_poll_ceiling="${1:?Poll ceiling required}";    shift
124	  local -r z_subs_file="${1:?Substitutions file required}"; shift
125	  local -r z_temp_prefix="${1:?Temp prefix required}";      shift
126	  test "$#" -ge 1 || buc_die "zrbld_spine_dispatch: recipe requires at least one step row"
127	
128	  test -s "${z_subs_file}" || buc_die "Substitutions file missing or empty: ${z_subs_file}"
129	
130	  # Read the substitutions blob's keys once for the dispatch-time coverage check;
131	  # the per-step scan rides the composition loop below, where each step body is
132	  # already include-expanded (zrbld_spine_validate). A JSON object cannot carry
133	  # duplicate keys, so no dedup is needed.
134	  local -r z_keys_file="${z_temp_prefix}subs_keys.txt"
135	  jq -r 'keys[]' "${z_subs_file}" > "${z_keys_file}" \
136	    || buc_die "Failed to read substitution keys from ${z_subs_file}"
137	
138	  buc_step "Composing ${z_label} Cloud Build steps from recipe"
139	  local -r z_steps_file="${z_temp_prefix}steps.json"
140	  echo "[]" > "${z_steps_file}" || buc_die "Failed to initialize ${z_label} steps JSON"
141	
142	  local z_row=""
143	  local z_script_path=""
144	  local z_builder=""
145	  local z_id=""
146	  local z_entrypoint=""
147	  local z_body_file=""
148	  local z_escaped_file=""
149	  local z_steps_built=""
150	  local z_body=""
151	  local z_shebang=""
152	  for z_row in "$@"; do
153	    IFS='|' read -r z_script_path z_builder z_id z_entrypoint <<<"${z_row}"
154	    test -n "${z_script_path}" || buc_die "Recipe row missing script_path: ${z_row}"
155	    test -n "${z_builder}"     || buc_die "Recipe row missing builder_image: ${z_row}"
156	    test -n "${z_id}"          || buc_die "Recipe row missing id: ${z_row}"
157	    test -f "${z_script_path}" || buc_die "Step script not found: ${z_script_path}"
158	
159	    z_body_file="${z_temp_prefix}${z_id}_body.txt"
160	    z_escaped_file="${z_temp_prefix}${z_id}_escaped.txt"
161	    z_steps_built="${z_temp_prefix}${z_id}_steps.json"
162	
163	    zrbfc_write_script_body "${z_script_path}" "${z_body_file}" \
164	      || buc_die "Failed to read step script: ${z_script_path}"
165	    zrbfc_expand_includes "${z_body_file}" "${ZRBFC_RBGJS_SNIPPETS_DIR}" \
166	      || buc_die "Failed to expand snippet includes in step: ${z_script_path}"
167	    z_body=$(<"${z_body_file}")
168	    test -n "${z_body}" || buc_die "Empty step script body: ${z_script_path}"
169	
170	    zrbld_spine_validate "${z_keys_file}" "${z_body_file}" \
171	      || buc_die "Recipe step '${z_id}' references a substitution register absent from the composition blob (see transcript)"
172

180	      || buc_die "Failed to write escaped step body for ${z_id}"
181

183	      --arg name "${z_builder}" \
184	      --arg id "${z_id}" \
185	      --rawfile script "${z_escaped_file}" \

200	    --arg       zjq_sa      "${z_mason_sa}" \
201	    --arg       zjq_pool    "${RBDC_POOL_TETHER}" \
202	    --arg       zjq_timeout "${RBRR_GCB_TIMEOUT}" \
203	    '{
204	      steps: $zjq_steps[0],
205	      substitutions: $zjq_subs[0],
206	      serviceAccount: $zjq_sa,
207	      options: {
208	        automapSubstitutions: true,
209	        logging: "CLOUD_LOGGING_ONLY",
210	        pool: { name: $zjq_pool }
211	      },
212	      timeout: $zjq_timeout
213	    }' > "${z_build_file}" \
214	    || buc_die "Failed to compose ${z_label} build JSON"
215	
216	  buc_log_args "${z_label} build JSON: ${z_build_file}"
217	
218	  rbrd_check "${z_token}"
219	
220	  buc_step "Submitting ${z_label} Cloud Build"
221	  rbuh_json "POST" "${ZRBFC_GCB_PROJECT_BUILDS_URL}" "${z_token}" \

223	  rbuh_require_ok "${z_label} build submission" "lode_build_create"
224	
225	  local z_build_id
226	  z_build_id=$(rbuh_json_field_capture "lode_build_create" '.metadata.build.id') \
227	    || buc_die "Failed to capture build ID from builds.create response"

18	#
19	# Recipe Bottle Lode - bole body (guard-free cluster, sourced by rbld0_Lode):
20	#   ensconce — capture an upstream base image into a Lode (Director credentials)
21	# The bole rides the capture-assembly spine (rblds_): this body owns only the
22	# kind-specific data — the ensconce recipe (skopeo capture + docker vouch push),
23	# the substitutions blob, and the per-member envelope extract loop — and composes
24	# them through zrbld_spine_dispatch / zrbld_spine_extract. No build-submission or
25	# step-composition machinery lives here.
26	
27	set -euo pipefail
28	
29	# Capture-file extension for buf_write_fact_multi (filesystem-as-data-bus).
30	# Pure literal tinder — one provenance fact-file per captured Lode, keyed by
31	# stamp: "<stamp>.${RBCC_fact_ext_lode}". Mirrors the roster fact precedent.
32	
33	######################################################################
34	# Internal Helpers (zrbld_*)
35	
36	# Internal: compose the ensconce capture recipe (skopeo capture + docker vouch
37	# push) and its substitutions blob, then ride the capture spine to submit and
38	# poll. The spine owns the capture-domain build knobs (mason SA, TETHER pool,
39	# regime timeout); this body chooses only the recipe, the substitutions, and the
40	# enshrine-borrowed poll ceiling.
41	# Args: token origin stamp
42	zrbld_ensconce_submit() {
43	  zrbld_sentinel
44	
45	  local -r z_token="${1:?Token required}"
46	  local -r z_origin="${2:?Origin required}"
47	  local -r z_stamp="${3:?Stamp required}"
48	
49	  buc_step "Constructing ensconce capture recipe"
50	  local -r z_gar_host="${RBGD_GAR_LOCATION}${RBGC_GAR_HOST_SUFFIX}"
51	  local -r z_gar_path="${RBGD_GAR_PROJECT_ID}/${RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY}"
52	
53	  # Recipe rows: script_path|builder_image|id|entrypoint, pre-resolved for the
54	  # spine. The | delimiter is load-bearing — builder refs carry colons (tags).
55	  local -r z_recipe=(
56	    "${ZRBLD_RBGJL_STEPS_DIR}/rbgjl01-ensconce-capture.sh|${z_rbfc_tool_skopeo}|ensconce-capture|bash"
57	    "${ZRBLD_RBGJL_STEPS_DIR}/rbgjl02-assemble-push-vouch.sh|${z_rbfc_tool_docker}|assemble-push-vouch|bash"
58	  )
59	
60	  buc_log_args "Composing ensconce substitutions blob"
61	  local -r z_subs_file="${ZRBLD_ENSCONCE_PREFIX}subs.json"
62	  jq -n \
63	    --arg zjq_gar_host     "${z_gar_host}" \
64	    --arg zjq_gar_path     "${z_gar_path}" \
65	    --arg zjq_lodes_root   "${RBGL_LODES_ROOT}" \
66	    --arg zjq_tag_bole     "${RBGC_LODE_TAG_BOLE}" \
67	    --arg zjq_tag_vouch    "${RBGC_LODE_TAG_VOUCH}" \
68	    --arg zjq_tag_digest   "${RBGC_LODE_TAG_DIGEST_PREFIX}" \
69	    --arg zjq_trust_grade  "${RBGC_LODE_TRUST_VERIFIED}" \
70	    --arg zjq_vouch_schema "${RBGC_LODE_VOUCH_SCHEMA}" \
71	    --arg zjq_acquired_by  "${RBGD_MASON_EMAIL}" \
72	    --arg zjq_origin_1     "${z_origin}" \
73	    --arg zjq_stamp_1      "${z_stamp}" \
74	    '{
75	      _RBGL_GAR_HOST:          $zjq_gar_host,
76	      _RBGL_GAR_PATH:          $zjq_gar_path,
77	      _RBGL_LODES_ROOT:        $zjq_lodes_root,
78	      _RBGL_TAG_BOLE:          $zjq_tag_bole,
79	      _RBGL_TAG_VOUCH:         $zjq_tag_vouch,
80	      _RBGL_TAG_DIGEST_PREFIX: $zjq_tag_digest,
81	      _RBGL_TRUST_GRADE:       $zjq_trust_grade,
82	      _RBGL_VOUCH_SCHEMA:      $zjq_vouch_schema,
83	      _RBGL_ACQUIRED_BY:       $zjq_acquired_by,
84	      _RBGL_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN:    $zjq_origin_1,
85	      _RBGL_IMAGE_2_ORIGIN:    "",
86	      _RBGL_IMAGE_3_ORIGIN:    "",
87	      _RBGL_LODE_1_STAMP:      $zjq_stamp_1,
88	      _RBGL_LODE_2_STAMP:      "",
89	      _RBGL_LODE_3_STAMP:      ""
90	    }' > "${z_subs_file}" \
91	    || buc_die "Failed to compose ensconce substitutions blob"
92	
93	  zrbld_spine_dispatch \
94	    "${z_token}" "Ensconce" "${ZRBFC_BUILD_POLL_CEILING_ENSHRINE}" \
95	    "${z_subs_file}" "${ZRBLD_ENSCONCE_PREFIX}" \
96	    "${z_recipe[@]}"
97	}
98	
99	# Internal: extract per-Lode provenance envelopes from the completed ensconce
100	# build and write one capture-file per Lode member. The skopeo capture step
101	# (step 0) authors the base64 JSON; the docker vouch step writes no output. The
102	# spine decodes step 0's slot to a file; the per-member slot_n envelope below is
103	# bole's shape, not the spine's.
104	zrbld_ensconce_extract() {
105	  zrbld_sentinel
106	
107	  buc_step "Extracting capture results from build step outputs"
108	
109	  local -r z_output_file="${ZRBLD_ENSCONCE_PREFIX}output.json"
110	  zrbld_spine_extract 0 "${z_output_file}"
111	
112	  buc_log_args "Ensconce output:"
113	  buc_log_pipe < "${z_output_file}"
114	
115	  local z_n=""
116	  local z_slot_key=""
117	  local z_stamp_file=""
118	  local z_stamp=""
119	  local z_vouch_file=""
120	  local z_vouch=""
121	  for z_n in 1 2 3; do
122	    z_slot_key="slot_${z_n}"
123	    z_stamp_file="${ZRBLD_ENSCONCE_PREFIX}${z_n}_stamp.txt"
124	    jq -r ".${z_slot_key}.stamp // empty" "${z_output_file}" > "${z_stamp_file}" \
125	      || buc_die "Failed to read stamp for ${z_slot_key}"
126	    z_stamp=$(<"${z_stamp_file}")
127	    test -n "${z_stamp}" || continue
128	
129	    z_vouch_file="${ZRBLD_ENSCONCE_PREFIX}${z_n}_vouch.json"
130	    jq -c ".${z_slot_key}.vouch" "${z_output_file}" > "${z_vouch_file}" \
131	      || buc_die "Failed to extract vouch envelope for ${z_slot_key}"
132	    z_vouch=$(<"${z_vouch_file}")
133	    test -n "${z_vouch}" || buc_die "Empty vouch envelope for ${z_slot_key}"
134	
135	    buf_write_fact_multi "${z_stamp}" "${RBCC_fact_ext_lode}" "${z_vouch}"
136	    buc_success "Ensconced Lode ${z_stamp} — capture-file ${z_stamp}.${RBCC_fact_ext_lode}"
137	  done
138	}
139	
140	######################################################################
141	# External Functions (rbld_*)
142	
143	rbld_ensconce() {
144	  zrbld_sentinel
145	
146	  buc_doc_brief "Ensconce an upstream base image into a Lode (parallel rbi_ld capture)"
147	  buc_doc_param "vessel" "Vessel sigil or path to vessel directory declaring the base ORIGIN"
148	  buc_doc_shown || return 0
149	
150	  # Resolve vessel argument (sigil or path) and load.
151	  zrbfc_resolve_vessel "${BUZ_FOLIO:-}"
152	  local -r z_vessel_dir=$(<"${ZRBFC_VESSEL_RESOLVED_DIR_FILE}")
153	  test -n "${z_vessel_dir}" || buc_die "Empty resolved vessel path"
154	  zrbfc_load_vessel "${z_vessel_dir}"
155	
156	  # Resolve the single base ORIGIN. Base-kind ensconce captures one base per
157	  # Lode per invocation; multi-base vessels are dispatched per base. Every real
158	  # vessel declares exactly one base slot today.
159	  local z_origin=""
160	  local z_origin_count=0
161	  local z_n=""
162	  local z_origin_var=""
163	  local z_slot_origin=""
164	  for z_n in 1 2 3; do
165	    z_origin_var="RBRV_IMAGE_${z_n}_ORIGIN"
166	    z_slot_origin="${!z_origin_var:-}"
167	    test -n "${z_slot_origin}" || continue
168	    z_origin="${z_slot_origin}"
169	    z_origin_count=$((z_origin_count + 1))
170	  done
171	
172	  test "${z_origin_count}" -ne 0 \
173	    || buc_die "Vessel '${RBRV_SIGIL}' declares no upstream base-image slot (RBRV_IMAGE_n_ORIGIN)"
174	  test "${z_origin_count}" -eq 1 \
175	    || buc_die "Vessel '${RBRV_SIGIL}' declares ${z_origin_count} base slots; base-kind ensconce captures one base per Lode — invoke per base"
176	
177	  # Reject producer-vessel pins: an origin naming a local vessel directory is a
178	  # made-side hallmark-pin (bind/airgap), not an upstream base to capture.
179	  test ! -d "${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}/${z_origin}" \
180	    || buc_die "Origin '${z_origin}' names a producer vessel — base-Lode capture is for upstream bases, not hallmark-pins"
181	
182	  buc_info "Ensconce base: ${z_origin}"
183	
184	  # Resolve tool images from reliquary (skopeo for capture, docker for vouch).
185	  zrbfc_resolve_tool_images
186	
187	  buc_step "Loading Director RBRA credentials"
188	  source "${RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source Director RBRA"
189	
190	  buc_step "Authenticating as Director"
191	  local z_token=""
192	  z_token=$(rbgo_get_token_capture "${RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE}") \
193	    || buc_die "Failed to get Director OAuth token"
194	
195	  # Mint the Lode stamp on the host: <kind-letter><YYMMDDHHMMSS>. The host owns
196	  # the stamp so the touchmark is known before the build for the capture-file.
197	  local z_stamp="${RBGC_LODE_KIND_BOLE}${BURD_NOW_STAMP:2:6}${BURD_NOW_STAMP:9:6}"
198	
199	  # Tweak override: test infrastructure pins the stamp via the bure tweak channel
200	  # to drive two captures onto one touchmark (the collision-guard exercise) — the
201	  # time-based mint is seconds-grained, so two CLI ensconces never share a
202	  # touchmark and the cloud guard's idempotent/collision branches never fire
203	  # without a pin. The name carries the buo sprue (BURE enforces the shape).
204	  # Mirror: rbtdrc_crucible.rs RBTDRC_ENSCONCE_STAMP_TWEAK_NAME — same literal.
205	  local -r z_ensconce_stamp_tweak_name="buorb_ensconce_stamp"
206	  test "${BURE_TWEAK_NAME:-}" != "${z_ensconce_stamp_tweak_name}" || z_stamp="${BURE_TWEAK_VALUE}"
207	
208	  buc_info "Lode: ${RBGL_LODES_ROOT}/${z_stamp}"
209	
210	  zrbld_ensconce_submit "${z_token}" "${z_origin}" "${z_stamp}"
211	  zrbld_ensconce_extract
212	
213	  buc_success "Ensconce complete: ${z_origin} -> ${RBGL_LODES_ROOT}/${z_stamp}"
214	}

201	  # GAR Categorical Namespaces (₢A_AAK layout)
202	  # Top-level namespaces under which arks are stored. Consumed by rbgl_GarLayout.sh.
203	  # The hm/rq/es siblings hold Director-authored image families; rbi_df holds
204	  # Payor-authored depot-scoped OCI artifacts produced during depot lifetime.
205	  readonly RBGC_GAR_CATEGORY_HALLMARKS="rbi_hm"
206	  readonly RBGC_GAR_CATEGORY_RELIQUARIES="rbi_rq"
207	  readonly RBGC_GAR_CATEGORY_ENSHRINES="rbi_es"
208	  readonly RBGC_GAR_CATEGORY_DEPOT_FACTS="rbi_df"
209	  readonly RBGC_GAR_CATEGORY_LODES="rbi_ld"
210	
211	  # GAR Lode Layout (fetched-side universal capture — see RBSL)
212	  # One Lode = one GAR package named rbi_ld/<kind-letter><stamp>; that package
213	  # IS the atomic-delete unit (single `packages delete`). Members and provenance
214	  # ride as TAGS within the one package, never as /-path-segments (GAR has no
215	  # subtree delete). Stamp matches the hallmark second-granular form YYMMDDHHMMSS.
216	  #
217	  # Kind letters (reserved set; only bole is implemented this pace):
218	  #   b  bole | t  tool | r  reliquary | w  wsl | vw  podvm-wsl | vn  podvm-native
219	  readonly RBGC_LODE_KIND_BOLE="b"
220	
221	  # Member/provenance tags. The rbi_ sprue marks strings from RB's domain:
222	  # RB's authored lexicon (bole, vouch) and RB-measured-from-content values
223	  # (the digest). It does NOT mark foreign-cued strings — the sanitized-origin
224	  # tag is UNSPRUED (origin is a vessel cue), computed at capture, not a constant.
225	  readonly RBGC_LODE_TAG_BOLE="rbi_bole"            # uniform greppable handle (bole singleton)
226	  readonly RBGC_LODE_TAG_VOUCH="rbi_vouch"          # one-per-Lode provenance envelope
227	  readonly RBGC_LODE_TAG_DIGEST_PREFIX="rbi_sha256-"  # canonical OCI digest tag: rbi_sha256-<full-hex>
228	
229	  # Provenance envelope (:rbi_vouch) — two honest trust grades, declared per Lode.
230	  # bole captures the durable-upstream grade; podvm-* will carry the recorded grade.
231	  readonly RBGC_LODE_TRUST_VERIFIED="verified-against-published"
232	  readonly RBGC_LODE_TRUST_RECORDED="recorded-at-acquisition"
233	  readonly RBGC_LODE_VOUCH_SCHEMA="rbld-vouch-1"    # near-term unsigned, schema-versioned
234	
235	  # rbi_df layout: flat namespace. No subdirs. Each filename names one
236	  # depot-scoped artifact; tag varies by artifact role.
237	  #
238	  # Current artifacts:
239	  #   probe-tether:probe   — tether pool levy-time capability probe
240	  #                          (cloud-pushed marker image, FROM scratch)
241	  #   probe-airgap:probe   — airgap pool levy-time capability probe
242	  #                          (cloud-pushed marker image, FROM scratch)
243	  #   rbrd:tripwire        — depot regime tripwire (FROM-scratch image
244	  #                          carrying rbmm_moorings/rbrd.env). Host-inscribed by
245	  #                          Payor at end of levy; pulled + byte-diffed
246	  #                          by every cloud-submitting command. See
247	  #                          Tools/rbk/rbndb_base.sh.
248	  #
249	  # The depot-time-immutable identity and pool settings (CLOUD_PREFIX,
250	  # DEPOT_MONIKER, GCP_REGION, GCB_MACHINE_TYPE) live in the RBRD regime
251	  # and are also inscribed into rbi_df at the rbrd:tripwire tag so post-
252	  # levy drift can be detected at every subsequent cloud submission.
253	  #
254	  # Enumerators (rbw-iah / rbw-iar / rbw-iae) ignore rbi_df by design — its
255	  # contents are operational, not part of the hallmark/reliquary/enshrine
256	  # image catalogue.

1	#!/bin/bash
2	# RBGJI Step 01: Inscribe tool images to GAR reliquary
3	# Builder: gcr.io/cloud-builders/docker (always pullable — Google-hosted)
4	# Substitutions: _RBGN_GAR_HOST, _RBGN_GAR_PATH,
5	#                _RBGN_RELIQUARIES_ROOT, _RBGN_RELIQUARY
6	#
7	# Mirrors all 6 GCB step/tool images from upstream registries to a datestamped
8	# GAR namespace (reliquary). Co-versioned: all images in one pass, one datestamp.
9	# Single-platform (linux/amd64) — tool images run as GCB steps on amd64 workers.
10	# Outputs JSON manifest with tool→digest mapping via /builder/outputs/output.
11	#
12	# Image URI shape: <host>/<path>/<RELIQUARIES_ROOT>/<RELIQUARY>/<NAME>:<RELIQUARY>
13	# Tag = the reliquary datestamp itself (immutable-datestamp-as-tag).
14	
15	set -euo pipefail
16	echo "=== Inscribe tool images to GAR reliquary ==="
17	echo "Reliquary: ${_RBGN_RELIQUARY}"
18	
19	# Tool image manifest: short-name|upstream-ref
20	# This is the authoritative list of tool images for GCB step execution.
21	# gcloud and docker are Google-hosted (gcr.io); rest are third-party.
22	MANIFEST=(
23	  "gcloud|gcr.io/cloud-builders/gcloud:latest"
24	  "docker|gcr.io/cloud-builders/docker:latest"
25	  "alpine|docker.io/library/alpine:latest"
26	  "syft|docker.io/anchore/syft:latest"
27	  "binfmt|docker.io/tonistiigi/binfmt:latest"
28	  "skopeo|quay.io/skopeo/stable:latest"
29	)
30	
31	RESULT='{'
32	FIRST=true
33	
34	for ENTRY in "${MANIFEST[@]}"; do
35	  NAME="${ENTRY%%|*}"
36	  UPSTREAM="${ENTRY#*|}"
37	
38	  echo "--- ${NAME}: ${UPSTREAM} ---"
39	
40	  DEST="${_RBGN_GAR_HOST}/${_RBGN_GAR_PATH}/${_RBGN_RELIQUARIES_ROOT}/${_RBGN_RELIQUARY}/${NAME}:${_RBGN_RELIQUARY}"
41	  echo "Dest: ${DEST}"
42	
43	  docker pull "${UPSTREAM}" \
44	    || { echo "FATAL: Failed to pull ${UPSTREAM}" >&2; exit 1; }
45	
46	  # Extract pulled digest from RepoDigests (format: repo@sha256:...)
47	  DIGEST=$(docker inspect --format='{{index .RepoDigests 0}}' "${UPSTREAM}" 2>/dev/null) || DIGEST=""
48	  DIGEST="${DIGEST##*@}"
49	
50	  docker tag "${UPSTREAM}" "${DEST}" \
51	    || { echo "FATAL: Failed to tag ${NAME}" >&2; exit 1; }
52	
53	  docker push "${DEST}" \
54	    || { echo "FATAL: Failed to push ${NAME} to GAR" >&2; exit 1; }
55	
56	  echo "${NAME} inscribed: ${DEST} (${DIGEST})"
57	
58	  # Accumulate JSON result
59	  if [ "${FIRST}" = "true" ]; then
60	    FIRST=false
61	  else

1	#!/bin/bash
2	# RBGJL Step 01: Ensconce a base image into a Lode (capture) via skopeo
3	# Builder: skopeo (from reliquary)
4	# Substitutions: _RBGL_GAR_HOST, _RBGL_GAR_PATH, _RBGL_LODES_ROOT,
5	#                _RBGL_TAG_BOLE, _RBGL_TAG_DIGEST_PREFIX,
6	#                _RBGL_TRUST_GRADE, _RBGL_VOUCH_SCHEMA, _RBGL_ACQUIRED_BY,
7	#                _RBGL_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN, _RBGL_IMAGE_2_ORIGIN, _RBGL_IMAGE_3_ORIGIN,
8	#                _RBGL_LODE_1_STAMP,   _RBGL_LODE_2_STAMP,   _RBGL_LODE_3_STAMP
9	#
10	# For each non-empty (ORIGIN, STAMP) slot: inspect upstream, measure the canonical
11	# digest, copy --all into ONE GAR package rbi_ld/<stamp>, then apply the member
12	# tags by GAR->GAR retag (dedups blobs). Author the provenance envelope and stage
13	# it for step 02 (the :rbi_vouch artifact) and for the host capture-file via
14	# /builder/outputs/output. Mason SA ambient auth via Cloud Build metadata server.
15	#
16	# Package shape:  <host>/<path>/<LODES_ROOT>/<stamp>            (one package = one Lode)
17	# Member tags on that package, all pointing at the base manifest:
18	#   :<TAG_DIGEST_PREFIX><full-hex>   canonical OCI digest (exact cross-Lode dedup)
19	#   :<TAG_BOLE>                      uniform greppable handle
20	#   :<sanitized-origin>-<sha10>      UNSPRUED — name + glance-fingerprint (= legacy enshrine anchor)
21	# The :rbi_vouch tag is a separate manifest pushed by step 02.
22	
23	set -euo pipefail
24	echo "=== Ensconce base images into Lodes ==="
25	
26	# Obtain OAuth2 token from metadata server (Mason SA) — shared library snippet.
27	#@rbgjs_include token-fetch
28	
29	# CB substitutions are expanded at submit time, not available as shell variables.
30	# Capture each into a runtime variable so we can loop.
31	SLOT_1_ORIGIN="${_RBGL_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN}"
32	SLOT_2_ORIGIN="${_RBGL_IMAGE_2_ORIGIN}"
33	SLOT_3_ORIGIN="${_RBGL_IMAGE_3_ORIGIN}"
34	SLOT_1_STAMP="${_RBGL_LODE_1_STAMP}"
35	SLOT_2_STAMP="${_RBGL_LODE_2_STAMP}"
36	SLOT_3_STAMP="${_RBGL_LODE_3_STAMP}"
37	
38	# Acquisition moment, attested once for the whole build.
39	ACQUIRED_AT=$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)
40	
41	# Stamp roster for step 02 (one line per captured Lode).
42	: > /workspace/lode_stamps.txt
43	
44	# Initialize result JSON (host reads this back as the capture-file source)
45	RESULT='{'
46	FIRST=true
47	
48	for SLOT in 1 2 3; do
49	  case "${SLOT}" in
50	    1) ORIGIN="${SLOT_1_ORIGIN}"; STAMP="${SLOT_1_STAMP}" ;;
51	    2) ORIGIN="${SLOT_2_ORIGIN}"; STAMP="${SLOT_2_STAMP}" ;;
52	    3) ORIGIN="${SLOT_3_ORIGIN}"; STAMP="${SLOT_3_STAMP}" ;;
53	  esac
54	  test -n "${ORIGIN}" || continue
55	  test -n "${STAMP}"  || { echo "FATAL: slot ${SLOT} has ORIGIN but no STAMP" >&2; exit 1; }
56	
57	  echo "--- Slot ${SLOT}: ${ORIGIN} -> ${_RBGL_LODES_ROOT}/${STAMP} ---"
58	
59	  # Inspect upstream, take the canonical digest, derive the glance fingerprint —
60	  # shared library snippet (requires ORIGIN + RAW_FILE; provides SHA + FINGERPRINT).
61	  RAW_FILE="/workspace/ensconce_raw_${SLOT}.json"
62	#@rbgjs_include skopeo-fingerprint
63	
64	  DIGEST_TAG="${_RBGL_TAG_DIGEST_PREFIX}${SHA}"
65	  PKG="${_RBGL_GAR_HOST}/${_RBGL_GAR_PATH}/${_RBGL_LODES_ROOT}/${STAMP}"
66	
67	  echo "Package: ${PKG}"
68	  echo "Digest:  sha256:${SHA}"
69	  echo "Tags:    ${DIGEST_TAG}, ${_RBGL_TAG_BOLE}, ${FINGERPRINT}"
70	
71	  # Collision guard — never silently clobber an existing Lode. The bole handle
72	  # (:rbi_bole) always points at this touchmark's captured image, so inspect it:
73	  # absent => a fresh touchmark; present => re-use. Re-use is legitimate ONLY when
74	  # it is the identical canonical digest (a Cloud Build retry re-copying the same
75	  # bytes); a different digest under the same touchmark is a clobber and fails loud.
76	  # The check sits immediately before the copy in this same step — atomic with the
77	  # GAR write in the sense the spec requires (no host pre-submit window). EXISTING_SHA
78	  # is computed the same way as SHA (sha256sum of the raw manifest), so the compare is
79	  # apples-to-apples. An inspect failure is treated as absent: cloud-side auth/network
80	  # is reliable, and a genuine GAR outage fails the copy below regardless.
81	  EXISTING_RAW="/workspace/ensconce_existing_${SLOT}.json"
82	  if skopeo inspect --raw "docker://${PKG}:${_RBGL_TAG_BOLE}" \
83	       --creds "oauth2accesstoken:${TOKEN}" > "${EXISTING_RAW}" 2>/dev/null; then
84	    EXISTING_SHA=$(sha256sum "${EXISTING_RAW}" | cut -d' ' -f1)
85	    if [ "${EXISTING_SHA}" = "${SHA}" ]; then
86	      echo "Touchmark ${STAMP} already holds identical digest sha256:${SHA} — idempotent retry, proceeding."
87	    else
88	      echo "FATAL: touchmark collision at ${PKG}" >&2
89	      echo "  existing :${_RBGL_TAG_BOLE} -> sha256:${EXISTING_SHA}" >&2
90	      echo "  refusing to clobber with  -> sha256:${SHA}" >&2
91	      echo "  (banish the Lode first, or ensconce under a fresh touchmark)" >&2
92	      exit 1
93	    fi
94	  else
95	    echo "Touchmark ${STAMP} is fresh (no existing :${_RBGL_TAG_BOLE})."
96	  fi
97	
98	  # Copy upstream into the Lode package under the canonical digest tag.
99	  skopeo copy --all \
100	    "docker://${ORIGIN}" \
101	    "docker://${PKG}:${DIGEST_TAG}" \
102	    --dest-creds "oauth2accesstoken:${TOKEN}" \
103	    || { echo "FATAL: skopeo copy failed for slot ${SLOT}" >&2; exit 1; }
104	
105	  # Apply remaining member tags by GAR->GAR retag (same blobs, manifest re-tag only).
106	  for MEMBER_TAG in "${_RBGL_TAG_BOLE}" "${FINGERPRINT}"; do
107	    skopeo copy --all \
108	      "docker://${PKG}:${DIGEST_TAG}" \
109	      "docker://${PKG}:${MEMBER_TAG}" \
110	      --src-creds  "oauth2accesstoken:${TOKEN}" \
111	      --dest-creds "oauth2accesstoken:${TOKEN}" \
112	      || { echo "FATAL: skopeo retag ${MEMBER_TAG} failed for slot ${SLOT}" >&2; exit 1; }
113	  done
114	
115	  echo "Slot ${SLOT} ensconced: ${STAMP}"
116	
117	  # Author the provenance envelope (identical content lands in :rbi_vouch and the
118	  # host capture-file). No jq dependency — values are controlled (sanitized origin,
119	  # hex digest, SA email, build id, ISO timestamp); none can carry a literal quote.
120	  # members[] is the cardinality axis — length 1 for the bole singleton.
121	  ENVELOPE='{'
122	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"schema\":\"${_RBGL_VOUCH_SCHEMA}\","
123	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"kind\":\"bole\","
124	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"lode\":\"${STAMP}\","
125	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"acquired_at\":\"${ACQUIRED_AT}\","
126	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"acquired_by\":\"${_RBGL_ACQUIRED_BY}\","
127	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"capture_build\":\"${BUILD_ID:-}\","
128	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"trust_grade\":\"${_RBGL_TRUST_GRADE}\","
129	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"signature\":null,"
130	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"members\":[{"
131	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"name\":\"${_RBGL_TAG_BOLE}\","
132	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"origin\":\"${ORIGIN}\","
133	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"digest\":\"sha256:${SHA}\","
134	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"verification\":\"oci-digest\","
135	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}\"tags\":[\"${_RBGL_TAG_BOLE}\",\"${DIGEST_TAG}\",\"${FINGERPRINT}\"]"
136	  ENVELOPE="${ENVELOPE}}]}"
137	
138	  # Stage the envelope for step 02 (pushes it as the :rbi_vouch artifact).
139	  printf '%s' "${ENVELOPE}" > "/workspace/lode_${STAMP}_vouch.json"
140	  echo "${STAMP}" >> /workspace/lode_stamps.txt
141	
142	  # Accumulate host-facing result (the capture-file carries the same envelope).
143	  if [ "${FIRST}" = "true" ]; then
144	    FIRST=false
145	  else
146	    RESULT="${RESULT},"
147	  fi
148	  RESULT="${RESULT}\"slot_${SLOT}\":{\"stamp\":\"${STAMP}\",\"vouch\":${ENVELOPE}}"
149	done
150	
151	RESULT="${RESULT}}"
152	
153	echo "=== Writing capture results ==="
154	echo "${RESULT}"
155	
156	# Write to buildStepOutputs channel (host extracts per-Lode envelope -> capture-file)
157	mkdir -p /builder/outputs
158	printf '%s' "${RESULT}" > /builder/outputs/output
159	
160	echo "=== Ensconce capture step complete ==="

1	#!/bin/bash
2	# RBGJS skopeo-fingerprint — inspect an upstream image's raw manifest, take its
3	# canonical sha256 digest, and derive the sanitized-origin glance fingerprint.
4	# The canonical digest is the sha256 of the RAW manifest bytes (what every tool
5	# reports); the fingerprint is the sanitized origin (`:` and `/` become `-`) plus
6	# the first 10 hex of that digest — the legacy enshrine anchor form. Only this
7	# inspect+digest+fingerprint core is identical across the skopeo captures
8	# (ensconce, enshrine); the first GAR copy is kind-specific (its destination tag
9	# is itself digest-derived) and stays in the step.
10	#   requires: ORIGIN    upstream image ref (e.g. docker.io/library/x:tag)
11	#             RAW_FILE  /workspace path to write the raw manifest into
12	#   provides: SHA          64-hex canonical digest (no algorithm prefix)
13	#             FINGERPRINT  "<sanitized-origin>-<first-10-hex>"
14	skopeo inspect --raw "docker://${ORIGIN}" > "${RAW_FILE}" \
15	  || { echo "FATAL: Failed to inspect upstream: ${ORIGIN}" >&2; exit 1; }
16	
17	# sha256sum (coreutils, not openssl) — runs inside the skopeo reliquary container.
18	SHA=$(sha256sum "${RAW_FILE}" | cut -d' ' -f1)
19	test -n "${SHA}" || { echo "FATAL: Empty digest for ${ORIGIN}" >&2; exit 1; }
20	
21	# Sanitize origin (: and / become -), append first 10 hex chars (legacy anchor form).
22	FINGERPRINT="$(printf '%s' "${ORIGIN}" | tr ':/' '--')-${SHA:0:10}"
23

55	_Trigger_: user demand for rootless deployment or macOS-native workflows beyond the current Podman VM approach.
56	_Reference_: RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc (current VM supply chain);
57	RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL (bottle lifecycle specs).
58	
59	**`podman-compose` lacks `--wait` for `compose up`.**
60	Docker Compose `--wait` (v2.1.1+) blocks until all container health checks pass, which `rbob_charge` relies on to guarantee crucible readiness before returning.
61	`podman-compose` does not implement `--wait` — the command returns immediately after scheduling containers, creating a race between charge completion and the active-check assertion in theurge.
62	Without `--wait`, Podman integration will need an alternative readiness gate (polling loop, explicit health-check query, or `podman wait --condition=healthy`).
63	_Trigger_: Podman first-class deployment (above).
64	_Reference_: containers/podman-compose#710 (June 2023, open);
65	containers/podman-compose#1329 (October 2025, open).
66	
67	**Revised enshrine — universal capture verb under Lode recipe noun.**
68	Today's narrow `enshrine` mirrors only upstream OCI base images.
69	Three additional capture needs share the same gesture (pull from upstream, SLSA-wrap, store in GAR): build-time tool images (today's `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery), the WSL substrate distro that hosts the Windows workload, and podman VM disk images.
70	The revision generalizes `enshrine` into a universal verb operating on a single recipe noun — **Lode**, parallel to {rbtga_vessel} on the built side — with a kind enum that gates pipeline behavior: `base`, `tool`, `reliquary`, `wsl`, `podvm-wsl`, `podvm-native`.
71	Today's `reliquary` demotes from top-level noun to a kind enum slot — the date-cohort semantic survives, the cult-vocabulary weight drops.
72	Today's `mirror` verb retires entirely;
73	absorbed into enshrine on the `tool` and `reliquary` kinds.
74	Lode .env carries author intent only — no resolved FQIN field — mirroring RBRV's ORIGIN/ANCHOR pattern (kindle code computes upstream coordinates from kind primitives at module-startup).
75	The `wsl` kind delivers a project-controlled WSL rootfs tar that replaces the Microsoft-Store-appx-mutable distribution today's caparison-w depends on;
76	the `podvm-*` kinds collapse `rbv_PodmanVM.sh`'s existing GHCR mirror into GAR under the unified verb.
77	_Trigger_: when Windows host workload requires a project-controlled WSL distro, when podman VM supply chain needs project-owned bytes in GAR, or when the `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery accumulates enough friction to motivate consolidation.
78	_Reference_: RBSAE-ark_enshrine.adoc (current narrow-scope spec);
79	RBSRV-RegimeVessel.adoc (ORIGIN/ANCHOR precedent, kind-enum-gated variable groups pattern);
80	`Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/FUTURE/rbv_PodmanVM.sh` (existing podman-vm fan-out machinery).
81	
82	**Hairpin peer access between crucibles.**
83	Bottled containers cannot currently reach each other's published entry ports.
84	Each bottle is isolated behind its own sentry on a separate enclave network.
85	The traffic path is feasible: Bottle A egresses through Sentry A's transit bridge, the host kernel routes to Sentry B's transit bridge, and Sentry B's existing entry path (DNAT + RBM-FORWARD) accepts the connection unchanged.
86	The security model is preserved — the sentry remains the sole enforcement point, with peer access as a new category of allowed destination alongside entry (inbound) and uplink (outbound internet).
87	Nameplate model: two new variables (`RBRN_PEER_ACCESS_MODE` disabled|allowlist, gated `RBRN_PEER_ALLOWED_MONIKERS` as comma-separated moniker list).
88	No global mode — peer access must always be explicit.
89	Implementation touches three areas: nameplate kindle/validate/render, a peer-access iptables step in security_config, and host-side peer IP discovery via container inspection.

1	= Podman VM Supply Chain
2	
3	== Motivation
4	
5	Podman is an excellent open-source tool, but its VM image distribution model creates a serious reproducibility hazard. After months of stable development using a particular Podman version, deleting and redownloading the VM with the same semantic version silently produced a fundamentally different image. The entire SENTRY/BOTTLE network architecture — which had been working reliably — broke. Virtual networking inside containers stopped functioning, and the failure mode gave no indication that the underlying VM image had changed.
6	
7	Systematic investigation (documented in Study/study-net-namespace-permutes/) revealed that the new VM image carried Podman 5.3+ security hardening that prohibited `setns()` on externally-created network namespaces. This broke Recipe Bottle's core isolation mechanism: containers could no longer join pre-configured network namespaces, regardless of privileges or creation method. The definitive post-mortem in Study/study-net-namespace-permutes/claudeopus4-netns-fail-memo.md confirmed this was not a configuration issue but a fundamental change in the VM's capabilities. The earlier networking journey is recorded in podman-gateway-proposal.md and the iptables architecture failures in Study/study-shared-pod-userid/ and Study/study-shared-pod-ip-forward/.
8	
9	The root cause is quay.io's build and retention policy. The upstream registry rebuilds VM images approximately every three hours and deletes older versions. Version tags are not immutable within a Podman semantic version — downloading the same tag at different times produces different disk images with different capabilities. There is no way to recover a previous image once it has been replaced.
10	
11	This motivated bringing VM images under user control by mirroring them into the project's own Depot artifact registry. By maintaining stable, versioned VM images in a controlled repository, Recipe Bottle can ensure reproducible environments and prevent silent regressions from upstream image churn.
12	
13	== Constraints
14	
15	Several technical and architectural constraints shape the VM supply chain design.
16	
17	VM images are OCI artifacts rather than standard container images. They contain compressed root filesystem tarballs for WSL environments or CoreOS disk images for standard virtualization platforms. These artifacts use non-standard media types, rely on custom annotations such as disktype to indicate their intended use, and carry opaque blobs rather than composable filesystem layers. While they are platform-indexed in the OCI sense, they are not platform-runnable as containers would be. Because of these differences, standard `podman pull` and `docker pull` cannot correctly retrieve and reassemble VM artifacts — they operate at the container-runtime abstraction level, which assumes runnable images with standard layer structure. Tools like crane (manifest and blob-level registry operations), skopeo (cross-registry copying with format awareness), and oras (OCI Registry As Storage, designed for non-container artifacts) work below that abstraction at the registry API and manifest level, which is why they can handle VM artifacts correctly.
18	
19	The bootstrap process faces a chicken-and-egg problem. The mirroring workflow requires crane, oras, or skopeo, but Recipe Bottle does not want to obligate users to install and maintain these tools on their host systems. The solution is to use an ignite VM, initialized with the latest uncontrolled upstream image, as a disposable tooling environment. Crane and jq are installed inside the ignite VM, and all registry operations execute there. The ignite VM is discarded after completing the mirroring operation.
20	
21	Establishing provenance for VM images is difficult. When podman machine init runs, it prints a SHA digest during initialization that can be captured and compared against expected values. However, this information is only available at initialization time. After a VM has been created, there is no reliable way to verify which specific image was used to initialize it. This limitation makes post-hoc auditing challenging and emphasizes the importance of capturing provenance information during the init operation.
22	
23	The technical constraint that VM images are locked to a specific Podman semantic version but not immutable within that version creates ongoing maintenance challenges. Two distinct image families exist: machine-os-wsl for WSL environments and machine-os for standard CoreOS-based systems. These families have different build processes, different artifact structures, and different platform variants, requiring parallel handling in the mirroring workflow.
24	
25	Storage cost considerations favor a conservative approach. Users do not frequently update their Podman VM installations, so maintaining a few full disk images in the Depot is practical. The storage expense is acceptable given the stability and reproducibility benefits.
26	
27	== Architecture
28	
29	The VM supply chain architecture centers on a two-machine pattern. The ignite machine serves as a temporary, disposable tooling environment. It is initialized with whatever upstream image Podman naturally fetches and is used only to run crane, podman, and other OCI utilities needed for manifest introspection and blob manipulation. The deploy machine, by contrast, is the persistent, user-controlled VM initialized from carefully selected and cached images stored in the Depot.
30	
31	The overall lifecycle follows a sequence of operations: check, mirror, fetch, init, and start or stop. The check operation discovers upstream updates by comparing digests between the current registry state and what is available upstream. The mirror operation queries upstream manifests for both WSL and standard image families, downloads individual disk blobs, repackages them as container images, and pushes them to the registry with platform-specific tags. The fetch operation pulls the platform-specific container image from the registry and extracts the disk image to a local cache directory. The init operation initializes the deploy VM from the cached disk image and writes a brand file containing full provenance information. Finally, start and stop operations manage the deploy VM's runtime state. A nuke operation destroys all VMs, both ignite and deploy, along with the local cache, enabling a complete reset of the environment.
32	
33	This separation between ignite and deploy machines provides a clear boundary between uncontrolled tooling infrastructure and the production-quality VM environment that hosts containers.
34	
35	== Operations
36	
37	=== Check (unimplemented)
38	
39	Discovers when new VM images are available upstream by comparing digest values between the current registry state and what quay.io currently offers. This operation was specified in earlier documentation but has never been implemented in bash.
40	
41	=== Mirror
42	
43	The most complex operation. Steps:
44	
45	. Bootstrap the ignite VM with crane and jq installed
46	. Query upstream manifests for both machine-os-wsl and machine-os image families at the specified Podman version
47	. Parse manifests to identify all available platform variants (architecture + disk type)
48	. For each platform variant, download the individual disk image blob from upstream using `crane blob`
49	. Repackage each blob as a container image: a simple Dockerfile copies the disk blob into a scratch-based container, with an identity file for traceability
50	. Build each platform-specific image locally within the ignite VM
51	. Push to the registry with tags encoding the Podman version, a timestamp-based identity, and the platform specification
52	
53	The result is a set of individually addressable container images, each containing a specific disk image for a specific platform variant.
54	
55	=== Fetch
56	
57	Pulls a platform-specific container image from the registry to the local workstation:
58	
59	. Start the ignite VM to access the podman image cache
60	. Authenticate to the registry
61	. Pull the platform-specific container image matching the user's station configuration
62	. Extract the disk image tarball from the container
63	. Write it to the local cache directory (always overwrites existing)
64	
65	The cached file serves as the source for subsequent VM initialization.
66	
67	=== Init
68	
69	Creates the deploy VM from the cached disk image:
70	
71	. Validate that the requested platform matches one of the available manifest platforms
72	. Run `podman machine init` with the rootful flag and the path to the cached image
73	. Start the VM temporarily to write a brand file containing the full provenance chain: Podman version, VM image origin, fully qualified image name, digest, and identity timestamp
74	. Stop the VM, leaving it ready for normal use
75	
76	The brand file provides an auditable record of exactly which image was used to create the VM.
77	
78	=== Start / Stop
79	
80	Straightforward lifecycle commands. Start brings the deploy VM online for container operations. Stop gracefully shuts it down. These do not affect the ignite VM.
81	
82	=== Nuke
83	
84	Complete environment reset:
85	
86	. Stop and remove all containers
87	. Destroy both the ignite and deploy VMs
88	. Delete the VM cache directory
89	
90	Requires explicit confirmation. Used when switching Podman versions, recovering from corrupted VM states, or performing clean test runs.
91	
92	== Configuration
93	
94	The VM supply chain relies on two categories of configuration variables. Regime configuration variables are stored in the repository and apply to all users working within the Recipe Bottle project. Station configuration variables are stored on each workstation and reflect local machine characteristics.
95	
96	The regime configuration includes several key variables. RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME specifies the name for the temporary tooling VM. RBRM_DEPLOY_MACHINE_NAME specifies the name for the persistent production VM. RBRM_CRANE_TAR_GZ provides the URL for downloading the crane binary into the ignite VM. RBRM_CHOSEN_PODMAN_VERSION identifies which Podman semantic version to mirror and use. RBRM_CHOSEN_VMIMAGE_ORIGIN records the upstream source registry. RBRM_CHOSEN_IDENTITY is a timestamp-based identifier assigned during the mirror operation to distinguish different builds of the same Podman version. RBRM_MANIFEST_PLATFORMS lists the platform variants that must be available in the upstream manifest and should be mirrored to the registry. RBRR_GITHUB_PAT_ENV provides the path to the credentials file used for registry authentication during mirror and fetch operations.
97	
98	The station configuration includes variables that vary by workstation. RBRS_PODMAN_ROOT_DIR points to the base directory where Podman stores machine data. RBRS_VMIMAGE_CACHE_DIR specifies where fetched disk images should be cached locally. RBRS_VM_PLATFORM identifies which platform variant the local machine should use, such as mow_amd64_wsl or mos_arm64_base.
99	
100	These variables are defined in the RBRM (Machine) regime at `rbmm_moorings/rbrm.env`, separated from the broader RBRR configuration to clarify the distinction between Recipe Bottle's application-level configuration and the underlying VM infrastructure configuration.
101	
102	An important transitional note concerns GHCR-era variables. The original implementation targeted GitHub Container Registry and used variables like RBRR_REGISTRY_OWNER, RBRR_REGISTRY_NAME, and RBRR_GITHUB_PAT_ENV to manage authentication and registry addressing. These variables are being deleted as part of ongoing work. When Google Artifact Registry support is implemented, new properly scoped variables will be minted following the project's prefix naming discipline.
103	
104	== Implementation Status
105	
106	The current implementation exists in Tools/rbk/rbv_PodmanVM.sh and targets GitHub Container Registry. This bash module implements the mirror, fetch, init, start, stop, and nuke operations as described above. However, the check operation, which would discover upstream updates by comparing digests, has no bash implementation and exists only as a concept.
107	
108	The codebase reflects an evolution well beyond the original specifications. Earlier specification documents RBSVC and RBSVM described a simpler design in which crane would copy entire images directly from upstream to the registry. This design envisioned variables like rbrr_chosen_vmimage_fqin and rbrr_chosen_vmimage_sha that would reference complete images. These variables were never implemented in code and remain spec-only ghosts.
109	
110	The actual bash implementation evolved to a more sophisticated approach involving blob-level extraction, repackaging of disk images as container images, support for both WSL and standard image families, iteration across multiple platform variants, and identity stamping for build traceability. This evolution reflects lessons learned during implementation about the structure of OCI VM artifacts and the need for fine-grained control over individual disk images.
111	
112	The vme.extractor.sh script was an earlier standalone tool for manifest introspection. It has been superseded by the zrbv_process_image_type function in the modern bash module, which integrates manifest parsing directly into the mirroring workflow.
113	
114	The file rbp.podman.mk contains historical Makefile prototypes from the GHCR era. These Makefile targets served as an initial exploration of the VM mirroring workflow but have been fully superseded by the rbv_PodmanVM.sh module, which provides a more robust and maintainable implementation.
115	
116	The implementation has never been ported from GitHub Container Registry to Google Artifact Registry. This migration remains necessary for production use within the Recipe Bottle architecture, which standardizes on Google Cloud infrastructure for its depot and service account management.
117

content.text:
This is decisive on two points:

1. **The verbs**: The agent named per-kind verbs "fetter" (tool), "conclave" (reliquary), "underpin" (wsl), "immure" (podvm), "augur" (read). Only **augur** exists in the codebase (RBSLA-lode_augur.adoc, rbld_augur). The others — fetter, conclave, underpin, immure — do NOT appear anywhere. These appear to be **agent inventions / hallucinations** presented as if they were established vocabulary ("verb fetter", "verb conclave", "verb underpin", "verb immure"). The kinds are real (tool/reliquary/wsl/podvm), but the per-kind verbs are not. This is the most significant error. It doesn't change the granularity recommendation (the recommendation is about kinds and their divergence, not verbs), but it's a load-bearing factual error in how the claims are framed.

2. **wsl trust grade**: RBS0:2187 defines `verified-against-published` as "bytes re-checkable against a persistent upstream (OCI content-address, **or an out-of-band published checksum**)" and explicitly says "**The bole kind carries this grade.**" So the published-checksum branch is a real, spec-named concept — but the spec attributes the verified grade to bole, and does NOT state wsl uses HTTPS+published-SHA. The agent's claim that wsl fetches "over plain HTTPS with a published SHA-256" is a reasonable inference (and consistent with the published-checksum branch), but it's the agent's design inference, not a spec fact. Notably the constants comment at rbgc_Constants.sh:230 says "podvm-* will carry the recorded grade" but says nothing about wsl's grade.

Let me confirm RBS0:2161 and 3863 (kind letters) and check whether RBSHR line numbers the agent cited (69-73, 75) are accurate, then I have enough.

67	**Revised enshrine — universal capture verb under Lode recipe noun.**
68	Today's narrow `enshrine` mirrors only upstream OCI base images.
69	Three additional capture needs share the same gesture (pull from upstream, SLSA-wrap, store in GAR): build-time tool images (today's `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery), the WSL substrate distro that hosts the Windows workload, and podman VM disk images.
70	The revision generalizes `enshrine` into a universal verb operating on a single recipe noun — **Lode**, parallel to {rbtga_vessel} on the built side — with a kind enum that gates pipeline behavior: `base`, `tool`, `reliquary`, `wsl`, `podvm-wsl`, `podvm-native`.
71	Today's `reliquary` demotes from top-level noun to a kind enum slot — the date-cohort semantic survives, the cult-vocabulary weight drops.
72	Today's `mirror` verb retires entirely;
73	absorbed into enshrine on the `tool` and `reliquary` kinds.
74	Lode .env carries author intent only — no resolved FQIN field — mirroring RBRV's ORIGIN/ANCHOR pattern (kindle code computes upstream coordinates from kind primitives at module-startup).
75	The `wsl` kind delivers a project-controlled WSL rootfs tar that replaces the Microsoft-Store-appx-mutable distribution today's caparison-w depends on;
76	the `podvm-*` kinds collapse `rbv_PodmanVM.sh`'s existing GHCR mirror into GAR under the unified verb.
77	_Trigger_: when Windows host workload requires a project-controlled WSL distro, when podman VM supply chain needs project-owned bytes in GAR, or when the `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery accumulates enough friction to motivate consolidation.
78	_Reference_: RBSAE-ark_enshrine.adoc (current narrow-scope spec);

2183	`members[]` is the cardinality axis: length 1 for singletons (bole), N for cohorts.

2186	+
2187	* `verified-against-published` — bytes re-checkable against a persistent upstream (OCI content-address, or an out-of-band published checksum). The bole kind carries this grade.
2188	* `recorded-at-acquisition` — upstream offers no durable re-checkable reference (e.g. quay rotates podvm images out within days), so RB attests only the digest observed at capture. Reserved for the podvm kinds.

content.text:
This is the final decisive evidence on the trust-grade axis. RBS0:2185-2188 says:
- `verified-against-published`: "The **bole** kind carries this grade." (only bole named)
- `recorded-at-acquisition`: "**Reserved for the podvm kinds.**" (only podvm named)

The spec does NOT classify tool, reliquary, or wsl into either grade. The agent's claim 7 asserts a clean two-way split: "bole and wsl can be verified-against-published; tool, reliquary, and podvm are recorded-at-acquisition." The spec only commits bole→verified and podvm→recorded. The placement of tool, reliquary, and wsl into trust grades is the **agent's inference**, not spec fact. The agent's specific assignments (tool/reliquary→recorded, wsl→verified) are plausible design reasoning but are presented with "high confidence" as if grounded.

Also note RBSHR citations: the agent cited "RBSHR:69-73" for the kind enum and reliquary demotion — the kind enum is actually at line 70, reliquary demotion at line 71. Close but the agent's quoted span 69-73 brackets it. The wsl citation at line 75 is exact.

I now have everything needed. Let me also quickly confirm the divine kind-letter and the "two distinct kind letters vw/vn vs single RBLDV code prefix" nuance is captured, and that there's no `rbld_augur` implementation gap relevant to claim 8's "augur" being kind-agnostic.

The recommendation's core logic (group tool+reliquary, separate wsl, split podvm into ≥2) rests on: (a) tool/reliquary sharing docker-pull/recorded-grade/same-source — well supported by rbgji01 + rbfli; (b) wsl being a registry-pull outlier — supported by RBSHR:75 (rootfs tar) + the registry-only skopeo snippet; (c) podvm being crane-based/two-family/fan-out — strongly supported by RBSPV + rbv_PodmanVM.sh. These structural facts hold regardless of the verb-name and trust-grade-attribution errors. Let me finalize.

input.corrections:
The granularity RECOMMENDATION stands — group tool+reliquary, one pace for wsl, split podvm into >=2, ~5 build paces total. That conclusion rests on the registry-pull-vs-crane-vs-tarball structural facts, all of which I verified hold (rbgji01/rbfli for tool+reliquary sharing docker-pull+cohort framing; RBSHR:75 + the registry-only skopeo snippet for wsl being a tarball outlier; RBSPV + rbv_PodmanVM.sh for podvm's crane/two-family/fan-out weight). None of the errors below move the cut lines.

But several load-bearing FRAMING facts in the claims are wrong or unsupported, and the operator should not carry them forward as established vocabulary:

1. INVENTED PER-KIND VERBS (most serious). The agent labels each kind with a verb: tool='fetter', reliquary='conclave', wsl='underpin', podvm='immure'. NONE of these four verbs exist anywhere in Tools/rbk — I grepped. The only real read verb is 'augur' (RBSLA-lode_augur.adoc). The kind NAMES (base/tool/reliquary/wsl/podvm-wsl/podvm-native) are real and spec'd (RBSHR:70, RBS0:2161); the per-kind capture VERBS are agent fabrication presented as if canonical ('verb fetter', 'verb conclave', etc.). This doesn't change granularity but would pollute the docket vocabulary if quoted.

2. TRUST-GRADE OVER-ASSIGNMENT (claim 7, marked false above). The spec commits only bole->verified-against-published and podvm->recorded-at-acquisition (RBS0:2187-2188; rbgc_Constants.sh:230 comment). Tool, reliquary, and wsl are UNASSIGNED in spec. The agent's clean 5-kind partition — and specifically wsl=verified-against-published and tool/reliquary=recorded-at-acquisition — is design inference stated at 'high confidence.' Flag it as a decision still open, not a settled fact, when these kinds are built.

3. wsl FETCH MECHANISM is inference, not spec (claim 5). 'plain HTTPS with a published SHA-256' appears in no cited source; RBSHR:75 says only 'rootfs tar.' The conclusion that wsl is a registry-pull outlier IS supported (it's a tarball, not an OCI image); the specific curl+published-SHA mechanism is the agent's reasonable guess about how that tarball would be fetched. Treat the cloud-step shape as undesigned.

4. CITATION SLIP in claim 6: 'acronyms.md:218 kind letters vw=podvm-wsl, vn=podvm-native' — acronyms.md is only 146 lines; the vw/vn legend actually lives at rbgc_Constants.sh:218 (and RBS0:2161, :3863).
